perturbation.original_text: Both parties understand and acknowledge that the Confidential Information has been developed or obtained by stated parties by in vestment of significant time, effort and exposure, and that the confidential information is a valuable, special and unique asset of stated parties which provides each with a significant competitive advantage. Therefore, stated parties agree to hold in conf idence and not to disclose the Confidential Information to any person or entity without the prior written consent of each Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Both parties understand and acknowledge that the Confidential Information has been developed or obtained by stated parties. Therefore, stated parties agree to, within reason, hold in confidence and not to disclose the Confidential Information to any person or entity without what could be considered prior consent of each Party.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase "within reason" and "what could be considered prior consent" introduces ambiguity, making the obligation to protect confidential information less stringent and open to subjective interpretation.
perturbation.law_explanation: FUTSA requires maintaining the secrecy of trade secrets with reasonable efforts. Modifying the contract language to 'within reason' and 'what could be considered prior consent' weakens the standard of care and undermines the reasonable efforts required under FUTSA to maintain secrecy, thus contradicting the law.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 688 > Section 003 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 688 UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT Entire Chapter SECTION 003 Injunctive relief. F.S. 688.003 688.003 Injunctive relief. — (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History. — s. 3, ch. 88-254.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity with phrases like "within reason" and "what could be considered prior consent," weakening the obligation to protect confidential information. The Law Explanation states that FUTSA requires maintaining the secrecy of trade secrets with reasonable efforts, and that the modified contract language undermines the reasonable efforts required under FUTSA to maintain secrecy. Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses injunctive relief for misappropriation under Florida Statutes Chapter 688, indicating the importance of protecting trade secrets. By making the obligation to maintain confidentiality subjective, the modified text potentially contradicts the requirement to make reasonable efforts to protect trade secrets, making enforcement and injunctive relief less certain.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties, hereby irrevocably agree, an d guarantee each other they shall not, directly or indirectly interfere with, circumvent or attempt to circumvent, avoid, by -pass, or obviate each other's interest, or the interest or relationship between the Parties with other haulers, trucking companies, contractors on project, future contractors on project, carriers, prime contractors, sub - contractors, producers, sellers, buyers, brokers, dealers, , government departments, distributors, financial institutions, technology owners, developers or manufactur ers, to change, increase or avoid directly or indirectly payment of established or to be established fees, commissions, or continuance of pre - established relationship or intervene in non -contracted relationships that by -pass one of the Parties with any hau lers, trucking companies, contractors on project, future contractors on project, carriers, prime contractors, sub - contractors, producers, sellers, buyers, brokers, dealers, government departments, distributors, financial institutions, technology owners, d evelopers or manufacturers, corporations, producer, technology owner, partnership, or individual revealed or introduced by one of the Parties to one another in connection with any on -going or future transaction or project.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties agree to generally avoid interfering with each other's interests, but shall not be absolutely prohibited from engaging with other haulers, trucking companies, etc. as long as it isn't a blatant attempt to circumvent established fees, commissions, or relationships.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing the strong prohibition with a weaker 'generally avoid' and allowing engagement with other entities as long as it is not a 'blatant attempt to circumvent' creates ambiguity and weakens the non-circumvention clause. What constitutes a 'blatant attempt' is subjective.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Deceptive and Unfair Trade Practices Act (FDUTPA)
perturbation.law_explanation: While FDUTPA doesn't directly address non-circumvention agreements, weakening the non-circumvention clause to the point where a party could unfairly benefit from the other party's relationships, by interpreting 'blatant attempt' in a self-serving way, can be seen as an unfair trade practice, potentially contradicting the spirit and intent of the act.

Home > Laws > 2023 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIII > Chapter 501 > Section 204 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title XXXIII REGULATION OF TRADE, COMMERCE, INVESTMENTS, AND SOLICITATIONS Chapter 501 CONSUMER PROTECTION Entire Chapter SECTION 204 Unlawful acts and practices. F.S. 501.204 501.204 Unlawful acts and practices. — (1) Unfair methods of competition, unconscionable acts or practices, and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful. (2) It is the intent of the Legislature that, in construing subsection (1), due consideration and great weight shall be given to the interpretations of the Federal Trade Commission and the federal courts relating to s. 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act, 15 U.S.C. s. 45(a)(1) as of July 1, 2017. History. — s. 1, ch. 73-124; s. 1, ch. 83-117; s. 4, ch. 85-63; s. 2, ch. 90-190; s. 3, ch. 93-38; s. 2, ch. 2001-39; s. 23, ch. 2001-214; s. 2, ch. 2006-196; s. 4, ch. 2013-207; s. 5, ch. 2015-92; s. 4, ch. 2017-155.
perturbation.justification: The original contract contained a strong prohibition against interfering with each other's interests, while the changed text weakens this by stating the parties should 'generally avoid interfering' and allowing engagement with other entities as long as it isn't a 'blatant attempt to circumvent established fees, commissions, or relationships.' This creates ambiguity, making the non-circumvention clause difficult to enforce. The law explanation suggests this can be seen as an unfair trade practice under FDUTPA. While the scraped snippet doesn't directly mention non-circumvention agreements, it states that 'Unfair methods of competition, unconscionable acts or practices, and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful' (F.S. 501.204(1)). The change allows for a more subjective interpretation of what constitutes a prohibited circumvention, creating ambiguity, weakening a clearly articulated and enforceable clause. This subjective element introduces a potential pathway for unfair practices, directly contradicting the prohibition of unfair trade practices.

perturbation.original_text: The Undersigned agree that they shall not use proprietary information or trade secrets unknown to the general public belonging to the Providing Party for their own use or for any purpose, other than the purpose authorized in writing by the Providing Party to the benefit of the joint business effort of the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: The Undersigned agree that they shall try their best not to use proprietary information or trade secrets unknown to the general public belonging to the Providing Party for their own use, except perhaps for purposes which are arguably related to the joint business effort of the parties.
perturbation.explanation: The phrases "try their best" and "arguably related" introduces significant ambiguity, making the obligation not to use proprietary information much weaker. It allows for subjective interpretations of what constitutes a legitimate purpose.
perturbation.law_explanation: FUTSA prohibits the misappropriation of trade secrets. Changing the language to 'try their best' to avoid use and allowing use for purposes 'arguably related' to the joint effort, weakens the obligation to protect trade secrets, making it easier for a party to justify misappropriation and thus contradicting the protection afforded by FUTSA.

Home > Laws > 2023 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 688 > Section 002 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 688 UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT Entire Chapter SECTION 002 Definitions. F.S. 688.002 688.002 Definitions. — As used in ss. 688.001 - 688.009 , unless the context requires otherwise: (1) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) “Misappropriation” means: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: 1. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or 2. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that her or his knowledge of the trade secret was: a. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; b. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or c. Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Before a material change of her or his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) “Person” means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) “Trade secret” means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process that: (a) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (b) Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. History. — s. 2, ch. 88-254; s. 750, ch. 97-102.
perturbation.justification: The change introduces ambiguity with phrases like 'try their best' and 'arguably related,' weakening the obligation to protect trade secrets. This contradicts FUTSA, as it makes it easier to justify misappropriation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' as disclosure or use of a trade secret without consent, and the modified text creates a loophole where such use could be 'arguably related,' thus providing a potential defense against a misappropriation claim. The original contract created an explicit obligation, while the changed contract introduces subjective elements which could be viewed as improper means.

perturbation.original_text: The Undersigned shall make every effort to protect and secure the information belonging to the parties while in their possession.
perturbation.changed_text: The Undersigned shall endeavor, where practical, to protect and secure the information belonging to the parties while in their possession.
perturbation.explanation: Adding "where practical" introduces a subjective element, allowing the recipient to decide when protecting the information is feasible, weakening the overall obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: FUTSA requires maintaining the secrecy of trade secrets with reasonable efforts. Allowing the recipient to decide subjectively where protection is 'practical' dilutes the 'reasonable efforts' standard, and undermines the intended protection under FUTSA.

F.S. 688.003 688.003 Injunctive relief. — (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History. — s. 3, ch. 88-254.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates 'every effort' to protect information. The changed text adds 'where practical,' introducing a subjective element that weakens this obligation. The Law Explanation states that this undermines the 'reasonable efforts' standard required by FUTSA. The scraped law snippet from the 2011 Florida Statutes, Title XXXIX, Chapter 688, Section 003, concerns injunctive relief for misappropriation of trade secrets. While the snippet itself doesn't define 'reasonable efforts,' the Law Explanation's argument, combined with the weakening of the obligation in the changed text, indicates a contradiction. By allowing subjective interpretation of 'practical,' the contract potentially falls short of the 'reasonable efforts' required to maintain trade secret protection, making injunctive relief less likely, thus creating a contradiction.
